selected_sources.title: GOV.UK Sanctions End-Use Controls Guidance for Businesses
selected_sources.snippet: We’d like to set additional cookies to understand how you use GOV.UK, remember your settings and improve government services. # Sanctions End-Use Controls: guidance for businesses. This publication is available at https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/sanctions-end-use-controls-guidance-for-businesses/sanctions-end-use-controls-guidance-for-businesses. This publication is intended to help UK businesses understand Sanctions End-Use Controls that have been introduced in relevant sanctions legislation containing export prohibitions, and to support exporters to third countries where there is a high risk of diversion of goods and related technology to a sanctioned destination or person. Sanctions End-Use Controls constitute a new licensing requirement for export to a non-sanctioned third country where the exporter has been informed by the government that there is a risk of ultimate diversion of the goods or related technology, via that route, to a sanctioned destination. Sanctions End-Use Controls give the government the power to impose a licensing requirement on UK exporters who wish to progress specific exports, once they have been informed by the government of a high risk of the goods and related technologies being diverted to a sanctioned person or destination.

selected_sources.title: ICC Academy Sanctions and Letters of Credit: What Banks Must Know
selected_sources.snippet: # Sanctions and letters of credit: Lessons for trade finance practitioners. *In this article, guest contributor Cayle Lupton discusses the legal and practical implications of financial sanctions on letters of credit, drawing on two landmark appellate decisions from England and Singapore.*. *The views and opinions expressed in this article are those of the author for educational purposes and do not necessarily reflect the official policy or position of ICC Academy or the International Chamber of Commerce.*. The result is a tension between the certainty and predictability of letter-of-credit undertakings and the obligation of financial institutions to comply with domestic and foreign sanctions regimes. Two recent appellate decisions – *Celestial Aviation Services Ltd v UniCredit Bank GmbH* [2024] EWCA Civ 628 (in England) and *Kuvera Resources Pte Ltd v JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A.* [2023] SGCA 28 (in Singapore)) – illustrate the legal and practical implications of sanctions on trade finance practice.

selected_sources.title: OFSI Strategy 2026-29
selected_sources.snippet: Blog: Office of Financial Sanctions Implementation. :   Office of Financial Sanctions Implementation. https://ofsi.blog.gov.uk/2026/04/15/ofsi-strategy-2026-29/. # OFSI Strategy 2026-29. This year marks ten years since the Office of Financial Sanctions Implementation (OFSI) was established. This Strategy marks the start of OFSI’s second decade, setting out an ambitious agenda for 2026–29 to keep UK financial sanctions effective, resilient and impactful. We will focus our policy and operational decisions—and our resources—on the areas of greatest impact, **grounded in high-quality data, evidence and a stronger understanding of threats, risks and context.** We will deliver timely and proportionate licensing, proactive and impactful enforcement, robust and targeted counterterrorism designations, and focused compliance support in our highest impact areas. We will work in close **partnership** across government, with regulators and law enforcement, and with the private sector and international allies to strengthen the impact of UK financial sanctions and support effective compliance. Through these partnerships, we will improve the quality of compliance support, target our operational activity more effectively, and amplify the deterrent effect of enforcement.

selected_sources.snippet: WHAT: Activity or information connected with the SCF program WHEN: Pre or post transaction Deal structures • Beyond capacity and or substance of customer • Improbable goods, quantities, origins, destination • Applicable import or export controls regulations may not be complied with • Countries / Names on the Bank’s high risk, Sanctions or terrorist list • Any attempt to disguise or circumvent countries involved in the actual trade • Unusual complexity and or unconventional use of financial products • Evasive behaviour of Party X or Party Y when being on-boarded • Or a combination of the above PRE Deal structures • Party X or Y changes its name, legal entity structure, accounting standards, country of incorporation or operation, or Bank relationship • New regions, currencies, Parties (X or Y) added after the initial setup • Or a combination of the above POST Transactions • Blatant anomalies or changes in value or volume of the transactions processed against the agreed / expected or historic value or volume • Changes in financing behaviour of Party X or Party Y • Changes in settlement behaviour of the buyer • Failed or returned payments to sellers (under a Payables Finance program) • Or a combination of the above POST PUBLIC 80 Trade Finance Principles Appendix V: FI Trade Loans 1.

This publication is intended to help UK businesses understand Sanctions End-Use Controls that have been introduced in relevant sanctions legislation containing export prohibitions, and to support exporters to third countries where there is a high risk of diversion of goods and related technology to a sanctioned destination or person. Sanctions End-Use Controls constitute a new licensing requirement for export to a non-sanctioned third country where the exporter has been informed by the government that there is a risk of ultimate diversion of the goods or related technology, via that route, to a sanctioned destination. Sanctions End-Use Controls give the government the power to impose a licensing requirement on UK exporters who wish to progress specific exports, once they have been informed by the government of a high risk of the goods and related technologies being diverted to a sanctioned person or destination.

rejected_sources.title: Sanctions Compliance in 2026: Controls, Screening & Governance
rejected_sources.snippet: # Sanctions compliance in 2026: How financial institutions are strengthening controls. Sanctions compliance in 2026 is more volatile, complex, and consequential than ever before. In mid-2025 it imposed composition penalties totaling **S$27.45 million across nine financial institutions** and a further S$960,000 on five major payment institutions for control weaknesses uncovered during inspections, underscoring the regulator’s drive to enforce robust financial crime controls beyond traditional banking risks. **Below, we examine the sanctions compliance weaknesses most frequently cited in enforcement actions and internal audits, and how institutions are addressing them in practice.**. Many enforcement cases demonstrate that sanctions controls were in place, but **institutions were unable to evidence that those controls operated as intended.** Issues frequently arise from poorly calibrated thresholds, ineffective name-matching or entity-resolution logic, limited linguistic coverage, or data-quality constraints that undermine alert decisions. ## Sanctions change management controls: Testing, validation, and deployment risk. In response, institutions are adopting **structured sanctions screening system testing** and validation frameworks that allow teams to assess the impact of changes before deployment and to evidence governance decisions after the fact.

rejected_sources.title: Sanctions Compliance in 2026: Controls, Screening & Governance
rejected_sources.snippet: # Sanctions compliance in 2026: How financial institutions are strengthening controls. Sanctions compliance in 2026 is more volatile, complex, and consequential than ever before. In mid-2025 it imposed composition penalties totaling **S$27.45 million across nine financial institutions** and a further S$960,000 on five major payment institutions for control weaknesses uncovered during inspections, underscoring the regulator’s drive to enforce robust financial crime controls beyond traditional banking risks. **Below, we examine the sanctions compliance weaknesses most frequently cited in enforcement actions and internal audits, and how institutions are addressing them in practice.**. Many enforcement cases demonstrate that sanctions controls were in place, but **institutions were unable to evidence that those controls operated as intended.** Issues frequently arise from poorly calibrated thresholds, ineffective name-matching or entity-resolution logic, limited linguistic coverage, or data-quality constraints that undermine alert decisions. ## Sanctions change management controls: Testing, validation, and deployment risk. In response, institutions are adopting **structured sanctions screening system testing** and validation frameworks that allow teams to assess the impact of changes before deployment and to evidence governance decisions after the fact.

rejected_sources.title: Sanctions in 2026 | Spotlight Blog | Themis
rejected_sources.snippet: # Sanctions in 2026. Rising geopolitical tensions, rapid technological change, and increasingly complex global trade flows are reshaping the risk landscape. But investment and professional services firms sit at a particularly sensitive intersection. Advising clients, structuring deals, forming entities, financing trade, or investing in emerging platforms can place firms directly or even indirectly in the path of sanctions evasion networks. In today’s world, sanctions risk is not just about what appears on a list. It is about intermediaries, new evasion tactics, and whole ecosystems designed to circumvent financial and trade-based restrictions. At Themis, we conduct research into sanctions circumvention to identify the latest tactics, typologies, and network structures being deployed across jurisdictions. This research feeds into our broader threat-based work, allowing us to track how evasion methodologies evolve and where enforcement and regulatory scrutiny are likely to move next. We’ve outlined the key threat areas reshaping exposure this year for investment and professional services firms, as we are seeing a clear uptick in sanctions-related risk across both industries.

This guidance is produced by the Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office (FCDO) to help businesses in third countries (not in the UK or Russia) that are seeking to avoid circumvention of UK sanctions. Since 1966, the Security Council has established 30 sanctions regimes. Each regime is administered by a sanctions committee chaired by a non-permanent member of the Security Council. rejected_sources.title: Sanctions Screening: 2026 Guide, Process and Best Practices
rejected_sources.snippet: Screen people, companies, vessels, and aircraft against global sanctions lists. Reduce false positives with stronger name, entity, and context matching. Search people, companies, vessels, passports, IBANs, and counterparties for MLRO review. Screen payments, transfers, counterparties, and transaction context. Upload CSV files to screen many people, companies, or records at once. Screen individuals and businesses during onboarding and review. Learn what sanctions screening is, when to screen customers and transactions, which lists matter, and how compliance teams reduce false positives. Sanctions screening is the process of checking customers, companies, beneficial owners, counterparties, vessels, aircraft, payment messages, and transactions against official sanctions lists. It helps compliance teams avoid dealing with restricted parties, detect financial crime risk, and keep auditable evidence of screening decisions. Lists change frequently, customer risk changes over time, and transaction context can introduce risk even when the original customer looked low risk. Screen people, companies, vessels, aircraft, payments, and customer files against global sanctions data with review workflows, ongoing monitoring, and audit evidence.

weighted_sources.title: Sanctions End-Use Controls: guidance for businesses - GOV.UK
weighted_sources.snippet: We’d like to set additional cookies to understand how you use GOV.UK, remember your settings and improve government services. # Sanctions End-Use Controls: guidance for businesses. This publication is available at https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/sanctions-end-use-controls-guidance-for-businesses/sanctions-end-use-controls-guidance-for-businesses. This publication is intended to help UK businesses understand Sanctions End-Use Controls that have been introduced in relevant sanctions legislation containing export prohibitions, and to support exporters to third countries where there is a high risk of diversion of goods and related technology to a sanctioned destination or person. Sanctions End-Use Controls constitute a new licensing requirement for export to a non-sanctioned third country where the exporter has been informed by the government that there is a risk of ultimate diversion of the goods or related technology, via that route, to a sanctioned destination. Sanctions End-Use Controls give the government the power to impose a licensing requirement on UK exporters who wish to progress specific exports, once they have been informed by the government of a high risk of the goods and related technologies being diverted to a sanctioned person or destination.

weighted_sources.title: Sanctions and letters of credit | What banks must know
weighted_sources.snippet: # Sanctions and letters of credit: Lessons for trade finance practitioners. *In this article, guest contributor Cayle Lupton discusses the legal and practical implications of financial sanctions on letters of credit, drawing on two landmark appellate decisions from England and Singapore.*. *The views and opinions expressed in this article are those of the author for educational purposes and do not necessarily reflect the official policy or position of ICC Academy or the International Chamber of Commerce.*. The result is a tension between the certainty and predictability of letter-of-credit undertakings and the obligation of financial institutions to comply with domestic and foreign sanctions regimes. Two recent appellate decisions – *Celestial Aviation Services Ltd v UniCredit Bank GmbH* [2024] EWCA Civ 628 (in England) and *Kuvera Resources Pte Ltd v JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A.* [2023] SGCA 28 (in Singapore)) – illustrate the legal and practical implications of sanctions on trade finance practice.

weighted_sources.title: OFSI Strategy 2026-29 – Office of Financial Sanctions Implementation
weighted_sources.snippet: Blog: Office of Financial Sanctions Implementation. :   Office of Financial Sanctions Implementation. https://ofsi.blog.gov.uk/2026/04/15/ofsi-strategy-2026-29/. # OFSI Strategy 2026-29. This year marks ten years since the Office of Financial Sanctions Implementation (OFSI) was established. This Strategy marks the start of OFSI’s second decade, setting out an ambitious agenda for 2026–29 to keep UK financial sanctions effective, resilient and impactful. We will focus our policy and operational decisions—and our resources—on the areas of greatest impact, **grounded in high-quality data, evidence and a stronger understanding of threats, risks and context.** We will deliver timely and proportionate licensing, proactive and impactful enforcement, robust and targeted counterterrorism designations, and focused compliance support in our highest impact areas. We will work in close **partnership** across government, with regulators and law enforcement, and with the private sector and international allies to strengthen the impact of UK financial sanctions and support effective compliance. Through these partnerships, we will improve the quality of compliance support, target our operational activity more effectively, and amplify the deterrent effect of enforcement.

Therefore, if you are providing technical assistance you should check whether the goods or technology may be used in Syria and [apply for a licence or contact DBT](https://www.gov.uk/guidance/how-to-apply-for-a-trade-sanctions-licence). For the purpose of the financial sanctions, the Regulations place obligations on [relevant firms](https://www.legislation.gov.uk/uksi/2019/792/regulation/70) to report information to HM Treasury (in practice to the Office of Financial Sanctions Implementation (OFSI), part of HM Treasury) about known or suspected designated persons or about persons who may have breached a prohibition or failed to comply with an obligation under specified provisions of the Regulations. The Trade, Aircraft and Shipping Sanctions (Civil Enforcement) (TASSCER) Regulations (2024) place obligations on[relevant persons](https://www.legislation.gov.uk/uksi/2024/948/regulation/15)(providers of financial or legal services, or money service businesses, for example)in relation to suspected breaches of certain trade sanctions.

transaction_decision_model.scenario_label: Illustrative sanctions-sensitive trade finance transaction screen

due_diligence_actions: Classify goods and check whether they are controlled, dual-use or sanctions-sensitive.
Screen buyer, seller, consignee, banks, intermediaries and beneficial owners.
Validate end-use, end-user and diversion risk with supporting documents.
Confirm licence, authorisation or exemption status before approval or drawdown.
Check payment route, correspondent bank exposure and blocked-payment risk.
Validate ports, vessel, transshipment and logistics route before financing.
Escalate unresolved red flags to sanctions/compliance and legal sign-off.
missing_documents: vessel, port and logistics route evidence
decision_rationale: High goods/end-use risk without confirmed licence or authorisation requires legal hold.
Missing or weak documents prevent routine approval.
Counterparty or ownership exposure is not clean enough for routine approval.
Unclear end-use requires enhanced due diligence.
refresh_triggers: Refresh if sanctions designations, OFSI guidance or export-control rules change.
Refresh if goods classification, licence position or end-use statement changes.
Refresh if counterparty ownership, payment route, vessel or logistics data changes.

likelihood.reason_for_score: Likelihood is driven by official UK sanctions/end-use guidance, controlled-goods risk, ownership/counterparty exposure and live route/diversion indicators.

impact.reason_for_score: Impact is driven by legal hold/rejection risk, payment blockage, documentation failure, credit exposure, insurance/underwriting exclusion and regulatory penalty consequences.

immediacy.reason_for_score: Immediacy is high because checks must be resolved before approval, drawdown, document honouring or payment execution.

exposure.reason_for_score: Exposure is proxied from impact where no separate exposure model is available.

confidence.reason_for_score: Confidence is capped because public evidence does not include transaction-specific goods, ownership, screening, route, payment, licence or document data.
confidence.reason_score_is_capped: Capped because Confidence capped at 3/5 because live public evidence is strong enough for a client-type screen, but transaction-specific goods, counterparty, ownership, route, payment, licence and document data are missing.
confidence.confidence_cap_reason: Capped because Confidence capped at 3/5 because live public evidence is strong enough for a client-type screen, but transaction-specific goods, counterparty, ownership, route, payment, licence and document data are missing.

decision_urgency.reason_for_score: Decision urgency is proxied from immediacy where no separate urgency model is available.

scoring_method_note: Structured analyst score with traceable evidence support; score_type distinguishes evidence-backed, analyst-assumption and illustrative-fallback scoring.
